events.label: reasoning
events.text:
The scenario describes pipe bedding & backfill work where lifts are too thick for the compactor. This is a compaction issue - backfill lifts must be placed in thin enough layers to achieve proper compaction. If lifts are too thick, the compactor can't properly compact the material, leading to settlement issues.

Lift thickness for pipe bedding/backfill typically should be 6-12 inches depending on the compactor type and material. If lifts are too thick, compaction won't be achieved throughout the lift depth.

This is a fail - non-compliant work. The lifts being too thick means proper compaction can't be achieved, which is a defect.

Let me construct the JSON response.

    "Backfill lifts placed too thick for the compactor in use — estimated lift thickness approximately 18 in, exceeding the maximum lift depth the compactor can effectively densify",

  "actions": [
    "Excavate and remove over-thick backfill lifts down to properly compacted grade",
    "Re-place backfill in lifts not exceeding the maximum thickness specified for the compactor type (typically 6–12 in for hand-operated equipment)",
    "Compact each lift to the specified density before placing the next lift",
    "Perform density testing per project specs before closing out the trench"
  ],
  "rationale": "Backfill lifts estimated at roughly 18 in are too thick for the compactor on site. A compactor can only densify material within a limited depth — typically 6 to 12 in depending on equipment. Lifts beyond that range leave loose, under-compacted material at the bottom, which will settle over time and can impose uneven loads on the pipe. This is non-compliant with project specs and IPC 306, which require backfill to be placed in layers of suitable thickness and compacted to achieve specified density. The work is rough-complete but defective and must be reworked.",
  "references": [
    "project specs — backfill lift thickness and compaction density requirements",
    "IPC 306 — trenching, bedding, and backfill installation standards"
  ]
